SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper clearly states its main claims and supports them with large-scale experiments (training on ~527k articles and a new 20k test set KP20k) showing CopyRNN outperforming classical unsupervised and supervised baselines; it also compares pure RNN vs. CopyRNN to demonstrate the copy mechanism’s effect. Mathematical formulation of the encoder–decoder with attention and copy appears standard and complete for the task. However, experimental methodology has some gaps from a reproducibility and rigor standpoint: the code/seeds are not mentioned, statistical significance testing is not reported, and it is unclear whether all baselines used identical preprocessing/vocabularies; these are minor but important for fully validating claims. Overall the conclusions are supported by the results, but better reporting of reproducibility and significance would strengthen the paper. | B(4): The paper's main claims—that a copy-augmented RNN (CopyRNN) improves present keyphrase prediction and can generate absent topical keyphrases—are supported by experiments on multiple established datasets and a new large test set (KP20k), with appropriate baselines (Tf-Idf, TextRank variants, KEA, Maui). There are no complex mathematical proofs to verify and the probabilistic training objective is standard and correctly applied; however the paper appears to omit statistical significance testing and deeper analysis of the reported ~2% OOV handling failures, which are minor issues that do not undermine the primary experimental findings. Overall the methodology is sound but would benefit from explicit significance tests and more exhaustive ablation on OOV/copy behavior.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper clearly states its main claims and supports them with large-scale experiments (training on ~527k articles and a new 20k test set KP20k) showing CopyRNN outperforming classical unsupervised and supervised baselines; it also compares pure RNN vs. CopyRNN to demonstrate the copy mechanism’s effect. Mathematical formulation of the encoder–decoder with attention and copy appears standard and complete for the task. However, experimental methodology has some gaps from a reproducibility and rigor standpoint: the code/seeds are not mentioned, statistical significance testing is not reported, and it is unclear whether all baselines used identical preprocessing/vocabularies; these are minor but important for fully validating claims. Overall the conclusions are supported by the results, but better reporting of reproducibility and significance would strengthen the paper.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main claims—that a copy-augmented RNN (CopyRNN) improves present keyphrase prediction and can generate absent topical keyphrases—are supported by experiments on multiple established datasets and a new large test set (KP20k), with appropriate baselines (Tf-Idf, TextRank variants, KEA, Maui). There are no complex mathematical proofs to verify and the probabilistic training objective is standard and correctly applied; however the paper appears to omit statistical significance testing and deeper analysis of the reported ~2% OOV handling failures, which are minor issues that do not undermine the primary experimental findings. Overall the methodology is sound but would benefit from explicit significance tests and more exhaustive ablation on OOV/copy behavior.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This work clearly sits within the scope of a top NLP/ML venue: it addresses an important NLP task (keyphrase generation) with a modern seq2seq architecture (bi-GRU encoder, attention, copy mechanism) and introduces a large training corpus plus a sizable new test set (KP20k) that will interest the community. The experimental setup compares to standard unsupervised and supervised baselines (Tf-Idf, TextRank, KEA, Maui, etc.) and reports macro-averaged precision/recall/F1, matching community evaluation norms. The problem formulation, methods, and evaluation standards are appropriate for the venue and target audience (NLP researchers/practitioners), and the paper is well positioned within related work on generation and extraction. From a venue-fit perspective the contribution and empirical scope make this a strong fit for a top conference. | B(5): This work sits squarely within top-tier NLP/ML scope: it advances sequence-to-sequence modeling for a core semantic task (keyphrase generation) and introduces a large training corpus plus a new KP20k test set that are valuable resources for the community. The method (encoder–decoder with attention and a copy mechanism) and comparisons against established baselines (Tf-Idf, TextRank variants, KEA, Maui) use evaluation metrics (precision/recall/F1) expected at these venues. The problem formulation—generating both present and absent keyphrases—is well-motivated for information retrieval and scientific text mining audiences, and the paper is positioned clearly within related work on copy mechanisms and keyphrase extraction, making it a strong fit for a top conference.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is generally well-organized and easy to follow (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early (CopyRNN, large training corpus, KP20k test set). Notation and methodological details (bi-GRU encoder, attention, copy mechanism, training objective, beam search) are described and hyperparameters are reported, and figures/tables appear informative given the effective comparisons to multiple baselines (Tf-Idf, TextRank, KEA, Maui). Minor presentation issues remain — e.g., code release is not mentioned and the discussion of OOV limitations could be clearer — but overall the writing quality and clarity are strong. | B(4): The paper is well organized with a clear flow from motivation to the CopyRNN method, experiments (on large training corpus and new KP20k test set) and conclusions; main contributions (attention+copy mechanism, large corpus, KP20k) are stated early. Notation and methodology (bidirectional GRU encoder, GRU decoder, attention, copy mechanism, beam search) are described consistently and hyperparameters are reported, and figures/tables (evaluation across multiple datasets and baselines) are informative. Writing quality is generally good and related work is thorough, though a few sections could be tightened for greater concision (e.g., more explicit pointer to code release or brief examples of failure modes).

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is generally well-organized and easy to follow (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early (CopyRNN, large training corpus, KP20k test set). Notation and methodological details (bi-GRU encoder, attention, copy mechanism, training objective, beam search) are described and hyperparameters are reported, and figures/tables appear informative given the effective comparisons to multiple baselines (Tf-Idf, TextRank, KEA, Maui). Minor presentation issues remain — e.g., code release is not mentioned and the discussion of OOV limitations could be clearer — but overall the writing quality and clarity are strong.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well organized with a clear flow from motivation to the CopyRNN method, experiments (on large training corpus and new KP20k test set) and conclusions; main contributions (attention+copy mechanism, large corpus, KP20k) are stated early. Notation and methodology (bidirectional GRU encoder, GRU decoder, attention, copy mechanism, beam search) are described consistently and hyperparameters are reported, and figures/tables (evaluation across multiple datasets and baselines) are informative. Writing quality is generally good and related work is thorough, though a few sections could be tightened for greater concision (e.g., more explicit pointer to code release or brief examples of failure modes).

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper tackles an important practical problem—automatic keyphrase generation—and demonstrates a clear improvement by adapting encoder–decoder models with attention and a copy mechanism (CopyRNN), showing superior performance on present-keyphrase prediction and the ability to generate absent/topical phrases. The assembly of a very large training corpus (~527k training articles) and the new KP20k test set substantially strengthens empirical claims and creates a valuable evaluation benchmark that other researchers can build on. While the lack of explicit code release is not stated, the methodological contribution (applying copy-enabled seq2seq to keyphrase generation) and scale of evaluation make this a strong, field-shifting contribution likely to be widely adopted and extended. | B(4): This work tackles an important, practical problem—automatic keyphrase generation—by framing it as sequence generation and adding a copy mechanism (CopyRNN) that demonstrably outperforms strong unsupervised and supervised baselines and can produce absent keyphrases. The assembly of a very large training corpus and the new KP20k test set provides a valuable resource likely to change evaluation practice and enable more robust models. By enabling generation of topical absent phrases and addressing OOV issues (albeit not perfectly), the paper opens meaningful follow-on research directions for generative keyphrase models and downstream applications in retrieval and summarization. These contributions make it a strong-impact paper likely to be widely built upon, though not necessarily transformative across all of NLP.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles an important practical problem—automatic keyphrase generation—and demonstrates a clear improvement by adapting encoder–decoder models with attention and a copy mechanism (CopyRNN), showing superior performance on present-keyphrase prediction and the ability to generate absent/topical phrases. The assembly of a very large training corpus (~527k training articles) and the new KP20k test set substantially strengthens empirical claims and creates a valuable evaluation benchmark that other researchers can build on. While the lack of explicit code release is not stated, the methodological contribution (applying copy-enabled seq2seq to keyphrase generation) and scale of evaluation make this a strong, field-shifting contribution likely to be widely adopted and extended.

reviewer_b.justification: This work tackles an important, practical problem—automatic keyphrase generation—by framing it as sequence generation and adding a copy mechanism (CopyRNN) that demonstrably outperforms strong unsupervised and supervised baselines and can produce absent keyphrases. The assembly of a very large training corpus and the new KP20k test set provides a valuable resource likely to change evaluation practice and enable more robust models. By enabling generation of topical absent phrases and addressing OOV issues (albeit not perfectly), the paper opens meaningful follow-on research directions for generative keyphrase models and downstream applications in retrieval and summarization. These contributions make it a strong-impact paper likely to be widely built upon, though not necessarily transformative across all of NLP.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The experiments are fairly comprehensive: large-scale training (≈527K articles), a new 20K test set (KP20k), and comparisons to a broad set of unsupervised and supervised baselines with P/R/F1 reporting, which supports the core claims about CopyRNN’s gains and OOV handling. However, ablation and sensitivity analysis appear limited — aside from noting that a pure generative RNN is overly general, there is no detailed isolation of attention vs. copy mechanism, beam/search or training-data-size effects. Related work is described as thorough and limitations (e.g., remaining OOV errors, ExpandRank scaling issues) are discussed, and hyperparameters are reported, but code/seeds and some reproducibility details are not mentioned. Overall this is a solid empirical study with minor gaps in ablations and full reproducibility. | B(4): The experiments are broad, comparing CopyRNN to multiple unsupervised and supervised baselines across standard datasets and a large new test set (KP20k), which supports the main claims; they also show CopyRNN vs a pure generative RNN, addressing the copy mechanism’s contribution. However, sensitivity analyses and extensive ablations beyond the copy vs no-copy comparison appear limited (e.g., impact of beam size, training set size, or variant architectures not reported). Related work is described as thorough, and the paper notes limitations (OOV handling, ExpandRank scalability) with some quantitative detail (~2% OOV cases), but deeper failure-mode analysis is sparse. Reproducibility is aided by reported hyperparameters and dataset sizes, though code availability is not mentioned, leaving minor gaps for exact replication.

reviewer_a.justification: The experiments are fairly comprehensive: large-scale training (≈527K articles), a new 20K test set (KP20k), and comparisons to a broad set of unsupervised and supervised baselines with P/R/F1 reporting, which supports the core claims about CopyRNN’s gains and OOV handling. However, ablation and sensitivity analysis appear limited — aside from noting that a pure generative RNN is overly general, there is no detailed isolation of attention vs. copy mechanism, beam/search or training-data-size effects. Related work is described as thorough and limitations (e.g., remaining OOV errors, ExpandRank scaling issues) are discussed, and hyperparameters are reported, but code/seeds and some reproducibility details are not mentioned. Overall this is a solid empirical study with minor gaps in ablations and full reproducibility.

reviewer_b.justification: The experiments are broad, comparing CopyRNN to multiple unsupervised and supervised baselines across standard datasets and a large new test set (KP20k), which supports the main claims; they also show CopyRNN vs a pure generative RNN, addressing the copy mechanism’s contribution. However, sensitivity analyses and extensive ablations beyond the copy vs no-copy comparison appear limited (e.g., impact of beam size, training set size, or variant architectures not reported). Related work is described as thorough, and the paper notes limitations (OOV handling, ExpandRank scalability) with some quantitative detail (~2% OOV cases), but deeper failure-mode analysis is sparse. Reproducibility is aided by reported hyperparameters and dataset sizes, though code availability is not mentioned, leaving minor gaps for exact replication.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares CopyRNN against a wide set of established unsupervised and supervised baselines (Tf-Idf, TextRank, SingleRank, ExpandRank, KEA, Maui) across multiple standard datasets and the new large KP20k test set using consistent P/R/F metrics, and it reports hyperparameters—supporting generally fair empirical comparison. The authors also discuss failure modes (e.g., ExpandRank scaling issues on KP20k) and the limitations of a pure generative RNN w.r.t. OOVs, and related work coverage is described as thorough. However, the summary does not indicate comparisons to contemporary neural or sequence-to-sequence baselines (other than the ablated pure generative RNN), nor is code/reproducibility explicitly provided, so a few relevant modern baselines and reproducibility details appear missing. | B(4): The paper compares against a strong set of classical unsupervised and supervised baselines (Tf-Idf, TextRank, SingleRank, ExpandRank, KEA, Maui) on multiple standard datasets (Inspec, Krapivin, NUS, SemEval-2010) plus the new large KP20k and reports consistent metrics (macro P/R/F1), which is appropriate. Comparisons appear largely fair (same data and metrics) and the authors explicitly discuss failure modes (e.g., ExpandRank’s scalability on KP20k, and limitations of a pure generative RNN without copying). However, while related work coverage is said to be thorough, the summary does not indicate whether the paper acknowledges any prior methods that might match performance or includes more recent sequence-generation baselines lacking copy mechanisms for direct ablation; thus I mark it as strong but with minor omissions.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares CopyRNN against a wide set of established unsupervised and supervised baselines (Tf-Idf, TextRank, SingleRank, ExpandRank, KEA, Maui) across multiple standard datasets and the new large KP20k test set using consistent P/R/F metrics, and it reports hyperparameters—supporting generally fair empirical comparison. The authors also discuss failure modes (e.g., ExpandRank scaling issues on KP20k) and the limitations of a pure generative RNN w.r.t. OOVs, and related work coverage is described as thorough. However, the summary does not indicate comparisons to contemporary neural or sequence-to-sequence baselines (other than the ablated pure generative RNN), nor is code/reproducibility explicitly provided, so a few relevant modern baselines and reproducibility details appear missing.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against a strong set of classical unsupervised and supervised baselines (Tf-Idf, TextRank, SingleRank, ExpandRank, KEA, Maui) on multiple standard datasets (Inspec, Krapivin, NUS, SemEval-2010) plus the new large KP20k and reports consistent metrics (macro P/R/F1), which is appropriate. Comparisons appear largely fair (same data and metrics) and the authors explicitly discuss failure modes (e.g., ExpandRank’s scalability on KP20k, and limitations of a pure generative RNN without copying). However, while related work coverage is said to be thorough, the summary does not indicate whether the paper acknowledges any prior methods that might match performance or includes more recent sequence-generation baselines lacking copy mechanisms for direct ablation; thus I mark it as strong but with minor omissions.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): From the summary, the paper adapts encoder–decoder models with attention and an explicit copy mechanism (CopyRNN) to the task of keyphrase generation and assembles a very large training corpus plus a new 20k-item test set (KP20k). Framed against prior extraction methods (Tf-Idf, TextRank, KEA/Maui) this is a notable and clearly differentiated application of sequence generation techniques to produce absent keyphrases and handle OOVs. While the core components (RNN encoder–decoder, attention, copying) are existing techniques, their combined application and dataset-scale evaluation represent a meaningful, non-trivial extension that would be informative to experts. | B(4): This work adapts encoder–decoder seq2seq modeling with attention and a copying mechanism specifically to the keyphrase generation task and pairs that with a very large training corpus and new large test set (KP20k), which together are a notable and useful advance over prior extraction-focused systems. The core modeling idea (attention + copy) is not novel in the abstract, but applying it to generate absent keyphrases and demonstrating clear empirical gains over classical extractive baselines represents a non-trivial, well-differentiated contribution. An expert would find the combination of method tailoring and large-scale dataset construction informative, though the technical novelty of the modeling components is moderate rather than groundbreaking.

reviewer_a.justification: From the summary, the paper adapts encoder–decoder models with attention and an explicit copy mechanism (CopyRNN) to the task of keyphrase generation and assembles a very large training corpus plus a new 20k-item test set (KP20k). Framed against prior extraction methods (Tf-Idf, TextRank, KEA/Maui) this is a notable and clearly differentiated application of sequence generation techniques to produce absent keyphrases and handle OOVs. While the core components (RNN encoder–decoder, attention, copying) are existing techniques, their combined application and dataset-scale evaluation represent a meaningful, non-trivial extension that would be informative to experts.

reviewer_b.justification: This work adapts encoder–decoder seq2seq modeling with attention and a copying mechanism specifically to the keyphrase generation task and pairs that with a very large training corpus and new large test set (KP20k), which together are a notable and useful advance over prior extraction-focused systems. The core modeling idea (attention + copy) is not novel in the abstract, but applying it to generate absent keyphrases and demonstrating clear empirical gains over classical extractive baselines represents a non-trivial, well-differentiated contribution. An expert would find the combination of method tailoring and large-scale dataset construction informative, though the technical novelty of the modeling components is moderate rather than groundbreaking.

main_claims: Proposes a deep keyphrase generation model (named deep keyphrase generation) using an encoder-decoder framework with attention and a copy mechanism (CopyRNN).
CopyRNN significantly outperforms both unsupervised and supervised baselines on present keyphrase prediction and can generate topical keyphrases that are absent from the source text.
Assembles a large training corpus (~567,830 articles, training on 527,830) and introduces a new large test set KP20k (20,000 articles) for comprehensive evaluation.
_summary.methodology: Use an RNN encoder-decoder (bidirectional GRU encoder and GRU decoder) with attention and an added copy mechanism (CopyRNN) to generate keyphrases from title+abstract; training maximizes conditional probability and uses beam search for generation.
limitations: Pure generative RNN (without copy) tends to produce overly general phrases and cannot recall out-of-vocabulary words.
ExpandRank fails to return results on KP20k due to high time complexity.
About 2% of correct predictions contain out-of-vocabulary words, indicating OOV handling remains a limitation.

reviewer_a.name: Methodology Maven
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer with 8+ years implementing and evaluating sequence-to-sequence models in NLP, focused on reproducible experimentation and robust empirical comparisons.
traits: Insists on clear train/validation/test splits, dataset cleaning procedures, and release of code and seeds for reproducibility
Requires thorough baseline comparisons and fair experimental setups (same preprocessing, vocabularies, and evaluation scripts)
Values ablation studies that isolate the contribution of each model component (e.g., attention vs. copy mechanism vs. decoding strategy)
Checks for sound evaluation practices including statistical significance, error analysis, and hyperparameter tuning details
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Detail-oriented and evidence-driven: concentrates on experimental rigor, reproducibility, and whether the empirical claims are justified by the presented experiments.

reviewer_b.name: Domain Impact Scholar
reviewer_b.background: Established NLP researcher specializing in information retrieval, keyphrase extraction/generation, and scientific text mining, with extensive experience assessing novelty and real-world applicability of models.
traits: Assesses novelty relative to existing literature (copy mechanisms, seq2seq keyphrase work) and whether technical contributions are incremental or substantive
Evaluates broader impact: usefulness of generated absent keyphrases, applicability to downstream tasks, and dataset representativeness for real-world use
Looks for thorough qualitative analysis (examples, failure modes) and discussion of limitations, including domain biases and potential misuse
Values clear motivation and positioning: why this problem formulation and dataset additions matter to the community
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Contextual and impact-focused: emphasizes novelty, relevance to the field, and whether the work advances practice or understanding in keyphrase generation.
